text: During the robotic right hemicolectomy, the surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the terminal ileum. The stapler's sensor ensures it is not fired across excessively thick tissue, and a yellow bar indicates the cutting action after staples are applied.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take down the mesentery, then attaches the terminal ileum to the transverse colon with a silk suture.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the end of the small bowel and the transverse colon, using Firefly imaging to confirm adequate blood supply to both sections before proceeding.

The surgeon fires the stapler to complete the side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis between the small bowel and transverse colon. The enterotomy is then closed using a running 3-0 PDS suture, starting at either end to prevent leakage. Intercorporeal suturing is performed to ensure there is no narrowing at the iliocolic anastomosis.
The surgeon places an additional row of Lambert sutures to reinforce the anastomosis. The defect in the mesentery of the small bowel is closed using a 2-0 Vicryl suture.
The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to check the anastomosis, confirming adequate blood supply to both the transverse colon and the ileum.
The surgeon removes the specimen using an endoscopic retrieval bag through a small incision, ensuring minimal impact on the surrounding tissue and achieving a cosmetically pleasing result.
